coredata.dc:title: Tailoring surface groups of carbon quantum dots to improve photoluminescence behaviors
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Ruixue
Hu, Shengliang
Wu, Lingling
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
Liu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: A facile and green method to tailor surface groups of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is developed by hydrothermal treatment in an autoclave. The photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of CQDs depend on the types of surface groups. Highly efficient photoluminescence is obtained through amino-hydrothermal treatment of the CQDs reduced by NaBH4. The effects of surface groups on PL behavior are attributed to the degrees of energy band bending induced by surface groups.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Surface state

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)–(d) PL behaviors of S1, r-GQDs, S2 and S3, respectively; (e and d) the comparisons of PLE and PL intensities of S1, r-GQDs, S2 and S3.
(a) EELS spectra of S2 and S3; the insets show the EELS spectra of C K-edge, N K-edge and O K-edge of S2 and S3. (b) FTIR spectra of S1, S2 and S3.
(a and b) The excitation wavelength dependent behaviors of S2-m and S3-m, respectively. (c) The comparisons of PL intensities of S2-m, S3-m, S2 and S3.
Schematic of the dependence of energy band bending on surface groups.
The comparisons of PL intensities of S3, S4 and S5 at the same excitation.

head.ce:title: Tailoring surface groups of carbon quantum dots to improve photoluminescence behaviors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China. Tel.: +86 351 3559638; fax: +86 351 35596381.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and green method to tailor surface groups of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is developed by hydrothermal treatment in an autoclave. The photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of CQDs depend on the types of surface groups. Highly efficient photoluminescence is obtained through amino-hydrothermal treatment of the CQDs reduced by NaBH4. The effects of surface groups on PL behavior are attributed to the degrees of energy band bending induced by surface groups.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the graphitic structure have received considerable attention as a new type of quantum dot because of their exceptional advantages such as large optical absorptivity, chemical stability, fine biocompatibility as well as low toxicity [1–4]. Unlike tradition quantum dots, their unique electronic and opto-electronic properties originate from structural defects since a perfect two-dimensional graphene lacks electronic bandgaps [1]. In fact, almost all of CQDs relevant to photoluminescence (PL) emissions can be observed structural defects though there are obviously many different ways to produce them [1–4]. It is totally confused that how the appropriate defects can be created in CQDs and why chemical functionalization can tune the bandgaps of CQDs. For example, PL emission from CQDs can be effectively improved and shifted by functionalizing them [5].

The defects in CQDs generally depend on the forming process (namely preparation method) and carbon source. For example, the amorphous structures are generally observed in CQDs from hydrothermal treatment of organic molecules while the structure of the products obtained from chemical oxidation and exfoliation of carbon fibers have less defects [6–8]. Specially, both methods show the different PL behaviors, suggesting the effects of structural defects on PL properties. However, it is presently difficult to control structural defects in CQDs only through one method. By contrast, tuning the bandgaps of CQDs can be more facilely realized through surface chemistry [1,9,10]. According to recent achievements [5,9–13], many elements (such as H, O, N, F, Cl) bonding with the surface of graphene and related materials give access to tunable electronic and optical properties. The progress for their underlying mechanism has been made so far. The suppression of the defect state emission was proposed to explain the role of surface functionalization in PL shift [10]. However, it is unclear now what the surface chemical structures are optimal for PL emission of CQDs and how they suppress non-radiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs. To clearly understand the effects of surface chemical structures on PL behaviors, in this work, we carefully designed the surface groups of CQDs and proposed the dependence of PL behaviors of CQDs on the surface group-induced band bending. Furthermore, a method to enhance PL efficiency of CQDs was developed.

The citric acid (1.05g) was dissolved in DI water (25mL). Then the solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50mL) and heated at 200°C for 5h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally. Thus, the transparent solution containing CQDs were obtained and named as S1.

The r-CQDs were prepared by reported methods [2,16]. Briefly, as-prepared S1 (10mL) solution was neutralized by Na2CO3 or NaOH. Then 50mg NaBH4 was dispersed in the neutralized S1 solution, and stirred gently overnight at room temperature.

The S1 solution (10mL) was mixed with 536μL of ammonia solution (28wt%) in a glass vial and subjected to ultrasonication (500W, 60kHz) for 15min, then the mixture solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50mL) and heated at 70 and 200°C for 5h, respectively. Cooling to room temperature, the light yellow solution (named as S2) was obtained at 70°C treatment while the dark brown solution (named as S3) was obtained at 200°C treatment.

The citric acid (0.42g) was readily re-dispersed in DI water (10mL) and then mixed with ammonia solution (28wt%, 536μL). After ultrasonic dispersion for 15min, the mixture solution was transferred to the poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclaves and heated at 70°C and 200°C, respectively, for 5h. Finally, the white transparent at 70°C (named as S2-m) and dark green solution at 200°C (named as S3-m) containing CQDs was obtained after cooling to room temperature.

The r-CQDs solution (10mL) was mixed with 536μL of ammonia solution (28wt%) in a glass vial. After ultrasonic dispersion for 15min, the mixture solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 5h. Cooling to room temperature, S4 was obtained. The prepared S3 (10mL) solution was neutralized by Na2CO3 or NaOH and then adding 50mg NaBH4 to the neutralized S3 solution. After gently stirring overnight at room temperature, one obtains S5.

Fluorescence and absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature on a Hitachi F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer and a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV/Vis spectrometer, respectively. Diluted supernatants containing CQDs were dropped onto copper grids covered with amorphous carbon film to prepare specimens for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation, which was performed in a FEI Tecnai G2F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200kV, and electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) analysis. FTIR spectra were recorded with a Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer.

Under hydrothermal conditions, many O-contained groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, or carbonyl groups, were formed at the surface of CQDs, thus imparting them with excellent water solubility (Fig. S1) [14,15,6]. It can be seen from Fig. 1a that as-prepared CQDs (named S1) show a typical excitation wavelength dependent PL behaviors and a larger full width at a half maximum (FWHM) (120nm) [15,6]. Under 365nm UV lamp, S1 exhibits very weak blue light emission. NaBH4 is a common and strong reducing agent that selectively reduces carbonyl and epoxy moieties to hydroxyl groups and significantly increases the amount of hydroxyl groups on the CQDs without reducing other species [2,16]. Thereby, it has been used as a facile and effective approach to enhance luminescent efficiency of CQDs. Fig. 1b shows PL behavior of the reduced CQDs (named r-CQDs). The PL emission also shifts with excitation wavelengths. PL intensity of r-CQDs is about three times higher than that of S1, and r-CQDs show the narrower FWHM (81nm).

PL behaviors of CQDs treated by ammonia solution depend on the reaction temperatures and PL efficiencies of all samples can be improved. Fig. 1c and d gives PL behaviors from the products obtained in two typical temperatures. PL emission from S2 shows a similar feature with S1 and r-CQDs, that is, the excitation wavelength dependent behavior and larger FWHM (96nm). However, PL property of S3 distinguishes itself from other samples. It shows excitation independent behavior at excitation wavelength from 320 to 400nm and the narrowest FWHM (65nm). Fig. 1e shows the PL excitation (PLE) spectra of S1, S2, S3 and r-CQDs. Their strongest peaks, which are also best excitation wavelengths for PL, are about 355, 360, 380, and 360nm, respectively. The strongest PL peaks from S1, S2, S3 and r-CQDs locate at 455, 450, 440, and 460nm, respectively, at the excitations of the strongest PLE peaks. The small gaps between the strongest PL and PLE peaks suggest less photon energy loss, facilitating intrinsic state emission [10]. Therefore, the highest PL efficiency is obtained from S3. At the same absorbance, PL intensity of S3 is beyond 28 times higher than that of S1 (Fig. 1e).

TEM images of all samples indicate that CQDs are dispersive and their sizes mainly range from 1 to 4nm (Fig. S2). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images suggest the lattice spacings of CQDs are consistent with the graphite structure and many defects are present in CQDs (Fig. S3). Interestingly, the sizes of CQDs in S3 is slightly larger than those in other samples, ex. S2, but S3 emit the shorter wavelength blue light. Obviously, there are little effects of the size on PL behaviors of CQDs. To find the critical factor that tune PL behaviors of CQDs, chemical composition and structure on the surface of CQDs were measured.

The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) results show that the O contents (atomic ratio) of S1, S2 and S3 are 18.53wt%, 14.89wt% and 7.03wt%, respectively; the N content of S3 (9.56wt%) is higher than that of S2 (5.27wt%). Further information of chemical structure on the surface of CQDs was provided by EELS as shown in Fig. 2a. The peak at 285eV is attributed to the transition from 1s to π* state and the peak at 291eV is the transition from 1s to σ* state [14,17]. These are the main EELS features of sp2 bonded (CC) carbon at the K-edge region. It can be seen from the enlargement of the N K-edge that S2 and S3 have different EELS signatures. The contribution at ca. 399eV can be assigned to pyridinic nitrogen, while the one at ca. 407eV is attributed to the formation of CONH group [17,18]. The peak at 539eV is caused by the CO bonding, suggesting that CQDs are functionalized by O-related groups [11]. FTIR spectra supported the above results (Fig. 2b). The various O-contained groups, such as CO, COH, and COC were observed in FTIR of S1. However, the COC group disappears in S2 and S3. Additionally, CO stretching vibration is absent in S3, but new peaks about amine groups, such as characteristic amide-carbonyl (CONH), CN in-plane, NH out-of-plane, and NH in-plane stretching vibration, are present in it [9,10]. These imply that ammonia solution reacts with epoxy groups to form a primary amine and alcohols by nucleophilic substitution [19,20]. However, the reactions between ammonia and O-related groups rely on the temperature on the basis of above analysis. With the increase of reaction temperature, more N-related groups are formed on the surface of CQDs while the O-contained groups decrease. Compared to COH group, COC group is easily extracted by ammonia. It is difficult to replace CO groups with amine groups under the low temperature.

Accordingly, the differences of chemical composition and structure at the surface of CQDs should result in the changes of PL behaviors. To verify the effects of surface groups rather than the inner structure, a comparative experiment was also performed. The mixture solution of ammonia and citric acid are used for preparing N-contained CQDs. Compared with S2 and S3, the obtained samples (S2-m and S3-m) exhibit different PL behaviors and lower efficiencies (Fig. 3 ). This implies that the samples obtained through ammonia participation of the nucleation process of CQDs are inferior to those obtained by the reaction between the synthetic CQDs and ammonia solution.

It is known that the PL emission of CQDs originates from structural defects [1]. Based on the calculation, the smaller sp2 clusters have a larger energy gap due to a stronger confinement effect [21]. We assume that the smaller sp2 clusters are easily present on the surface of CQDs. Therefore, surface groups at the sp2 clusters could affect both their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and their lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). As shown in Fig. 4 , the energy bands between HOMO and LUMO may be shifted continuously due to charge distribution difference between the inner and the surface that leads to the charge transfer [22]. The directions and degrees of energy band bending could be changed with the different surface groups. However, PL intensity or efficiency only depends on the band bending degrees (V). The large V will facilitate separation of the electron–hole pairs and induce faster carrier migration, reducing the ratio of direct band-to-band recombination of electron and hole [22]. There are some other defects or impurities in CQDs. The photon-excited electrons may also be trapped at these trap sites during their migration, thereby emitting the photon or releasing the heat. The differences of trap states result in a large FWHM and an excitation wavelength dependent PL behavior. On the other hand, the small V easily induces direct band-to-band recombination of photon-excited electron and hole, displaying a narrow FWHM, an excitation wavelength independent PL behavior and the emission with high energy photons. Specially, this case will improve PL intensity (I
PL ) on the basis of the equation [23]:
I
P L
∝
I 0
exp ( − α V )
, where I 0 is the incident light intensity, α is the sum of the absorptivities of the incident and emitted photons.

The unpaired electrons of the amine groups can contribute to the electron based on the electron-withdrawing and electron-accepting behaviors of surface groups to benzene, which are well-known behaviors in organic chemistry [5]. Electron transfer from amine groups to the sp2 clusters at the surface causes the increase of surface electron density, which could result in the lowering V. Hydroxyl groups are also electron-donating, so increasing their quantity by reduction can also enhance the PL emission of CQDs [16]. The charged electron of the carboxyl groups can weaken the electron-withdrawing properties [5], thus inducing a large V. Therefore, the carboxyl groups could reduce PL intensity on the basis of above discussion.

We developed a facile and green approach to prepare amino-functionalized CQDs. The N contents on the surface of GQDs are determined by the reaction temperature of amino-hydrothermal treatment. To further increase the amount of amine groups, an efficient method to treat r-GQDs with ammonia solution at 200°C was found. The samples obtained by this method can drastically improve PL efficiency of CQDs. The effects of surface groups on PL behaviors are attributed to their induced band bending degrees. The introduction of amine groups in CQDs can reduce the band bending degrees and then facilitate direct band-to-band recombination, resulting in high PL efficiency.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51272301, 51172214, and 51172120), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (Nos. 2012M510788 and 2013T60269), Program for the Top Young Academic Leaders of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, and State Key Laboratory of New Ceramic and Fine Processing Tsinghua University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2014.02.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dramatic fluorescence enhancement of bare carbon dots through facile reduction chemistry
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots through the charge transfer effect of functional groups
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
Blue and green photoluminescence graphene quantum dots synthesized from carbon fibers
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
Optically tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Bandgap opening in graphene induced by patterned hydrogen adsorption
Interfacing quantum dots and graphitic surfaces with chlorine atomic ligands
Anomalous behaviors of visible luminescence from graphene quantum dots: interplay between size and shape
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
Atomistic description of electron beam damage in nitrogen-doped graphene and single-walled carbon nanotubes
High-resolution electron energy-loss spectroscopy of undoped and nitrogen-doped tetrahedral amorphous carbon films
Mechanism of ammonia retention on graphite oxides: role of surface chemistry and structure
One-step synthesis of NH2-graphene from in situ graphene-oxide reduction and its improved electrochemical properties
Graphene oxide as a chemically tunable platform for optical applications
Band bending in semiconductors: chemical and physical consequences at surfaces and interfaces
Chemical regulation of carbon quantum dots from synthesis to photocatalytic activity
